cataloging_subject.label: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English verse Midland Version, Text D, of The Seven Sages of Rome, composed in the second half of the fourteenth century and uniquely witnessed in Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17, vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb. It excludes the wider multilingual Seven Sages / Book of Sindbad tradition, other Middle English redactions, the full manuscript miscellany, Thomas Wright's 1845–1846 editorial apparatus, Jill Whitelock's 2005 edition, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and digital scans as separate layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed 1350–1400; attested by a late-fourteenth-century manuscript and first known in print in Wright's 1845 text issue
catalog_summary.region: Middle English work with an English Midlands dialect association; exact place of composition unknown; frame narrative set in Rome

titles.value: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
notes: This is the established modern title for the cataloged redaction; it is not asserted as a manuscript rubric.

agents.name: Thomas Wright
notes: Editor of the 1845 text and 1846 introduction and notes, not the medieval author.
Current scholarship treats the Midland Version as an anonymous redaction or adaptation. The exact immediate source—lost Middle English parent or French prose A—remains unresolved.

Wright's responsibility is for the immediate 1845–1846 source edition. He states that he corrected some scribal errors sparingly, restored a detached article n, and separated words joined in the manuscript.

date_statements.display: The Midland Version is dated by current Whitelock-based scholarship to 1350–1400

assertion.notes: This is the scholarly date of the Midland redaction, not the earlier 1275–1320 range assigned to the broader Middle English Version A tradition.

date_statements.display: The sole surviving witness, Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17, is dated to the second half of the fourteenth century (1350–1400)

assertion.notes: This dates the witness and establishes attestation; it does not independently prove an exact composition year.

date_statements.display: First known printed as Percy Society publication no. 53 in January 1845, edited by Thomas Wright

assertion.notes: The 1845 issue and its use of Dd.1.17 are direct. 'First known' remains qualified because the inspected sources do not present an exhaustive publication census.

places.label: English Midlands
notes: A broad dialect and redaction label; no exact place of composition is claimed.

assertion.notes: The redaction belongs to the Middle English Version A tradition and its witness was produced in England. This does not establish an exact place of composition.
Midland is the established linguistic/redactional classification; it is not narrowed here to a modern county or settlement.

notes: Rome is the frame narrative's principal court setting; many embedded tales use other or generic settings.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English appears only in editorial and catalog apparatus and is not an intrinsic language of the medieval work.

form_statements.description: A 3,455-line Middle English verse romance, predominantly in rhyming couplets; the XML represents the opening frame and fifteen tale divisions in sixteen LG elements.

assertion.notes: The prince's silence and threatened execution frame alternating cautionary tales by the Empress and sages, followed by the prince's own tale.

subject_statements.label: False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

assertion.notes: The classification describes a demonstrable thematic and rhetorical pattern; it does not collapse every embedded tale into one identical moral.

subject_statements.label: Education, astrological foreknowledge, and enforced silence

summaries.value: A Roman emperor entrusts his son to seven sages. When the prince returns, astrological danger requires him to remain silent; his stepmother propositions him, then falsely accuses him after he refuses her. The emperor repeatedly orders his son's execution, while the sages and Empress alternate cautionary tales until the prince can speak, expose the plot, and tell a final story of a child who understands ravens.

The XML presents 591 opening-frame lines followed by fifteen tale-labeled verse divisions: the fourteen alternating Empress/Sage stories Arbor, Canis, Aper, Medicus, Gaza, Puteus, Senescalcus, Tentamina, Virgilius, Avis, Sapientes, Vidua, Roma, and Inclusa, followed by the prince's tale of the ravens and the frame conclusion.

The fourteen-name specialist story list and the XML's fifteen DIV2 elements are compatible modeling conventions: the XML gives the prince's final ravens story its own tale division, while the database lists the alternating Empress/Sage cycle. Generic printed heads such as A TALE are editorial labels, not recovered medieval story titles.

extent.description: The complete XML poem has 3,455 L elements in 16 LG elements: one opening frame group and 15 DIV2 tale groups. It also has 16 HEAD elements, 117 PB elements spanning the title page and printed poem pp. 1–116, and 342 MILESTONE elements for printed line numbers. These are encoding and edition metrics; the 15 DIV2 units include frame transitions and should not be treated as fifteen freestanding authored works.

content_parts.label: Opening frame narrative
content_parts.summary: The emperor, prince, sages, education, remarriage, astrological danger, return to court, accusation, and first threatened execution through the first demand for a delaying tale.
notes: The part boundary is the XML/edition boundary before the first DIV2 and does not claim that the manuscript supplied a formal heading at this point.

content_parts.label: Fifteen tale divisions and frame conclusion
content_parts.summary: Fourteen alternating Empress/Sage tales, the prince's final tale of the ravens, and the frame transitions and conclusion carried within the fifteen printed tale divisions.
notes: Successive divisions share printed boundary pages because a new tale can begin partway through a page.
The generic DIV2 heads are inherited from Wright's printed layout and do not name most stories.

related_works.title: Middle English Version A of The Seven Sages of Rome
notes: The Midland redaction securely belongs to Middle English Version A but is distinct from the Northern/Southern Y Group.
Whitelock-based scholarship leaves open whether its immediate source was a lost Middle English parent x or French prose Version A directly.

evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for the preferred identity, Middle English Version A / Midland Version / Text D hierarchy, folios 54ra–63rb, unique witness, production range, England origin, physical codex context, fourteen alternating named stories, and Whitelock edition.

evidence.citation: The Seven Sages of Rome Database, Midland Version.
evidence.locator: version description, date of composition, parent relationship, story sequence, and connected witness
evidence.summary: Whitelock-based specialist evidence for the 1350–1400 composition range, the sole Dd.1.17 witness, the Midland redaction's distinction from the Y Group, and uncertainty between a lost Middle English parent and direct French prose A source.

evidence.citation: Bonsall, Bildhauer, and Nöth, eds., The Seven Sages of Rome Database (2024–2026), About.
evidence.summary: Project evidence for the scholarly database's scope, team, funding, frame-tale definition, sources, conventions, and work-in-progress qualification.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, record 193818, The seven sages of Rome (midland version), edited by Jill Whitelock.
evidence.summary: Responsible catalog evidence for the established title, Jill Whitelock's direct edition from Dd.1.17, 2005 publication, EETS series, pagination, and Middle English text with English introduction and commentary.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CME00018.xml with the repository inspection script and XPath element counts.
evidence.locator: count(//DIV1), count(//DIV2), count(//HEAD), count(//LG), count(//L), count(//P), count(//PB), count(//MILESTONE), and TEXT/@LANG
evidence.summary: Reproducibly finds 2 DIV1, 15 DIV2, 16 HEAD, 16 LG, 3,455 L, 6 P, 117 PB, and 342 MILESTONE elements, with TEXT/@LANG='enm'. The first DIV2 begins after 591 L elements, leaving 2,864 lines in the fifteen divisions. These are encoding metrics rather than proof of an authorial section system.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of all three current volumes of Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17 be obtained so that the work boundary, foliation, hand, lineation, and Wright's readings can be checked directly?
next_steps: Ask Cambridge Special Collections about complete existing photography, legacy microfilm, or an internal surrogate.
Request every physical surface in all three volumes rather than only ff. 54ra–63rb, then map the work portion separately.
Confirm retention and reuse terms before caching repository-supplied files.
notes: The lineage manifest records the full dated search scope and mediated Cambridge imaging route.

open_questions.question: Was the Midland redaction adapted through a lost Middle English parent x or directly from French prose Version A?
next_steps: Consult Whitelock's full textual analysis and later stemmatic scholarship.
Keep the work related securely to Middle English Version A without inventing an immediate source exemplar.

open_questions.question: How do the 1856 count of 424 leaves and continuous pp. 1–848 map onto the current three-volume counts and foliation, including the work's current ff. 54ra–63rb?
next_steps: Inspect the current collation and foliation in a complete facsimile or onsite.
Consult modern codicological work on the manuscript's division and rebinding.
Preserve both attributed extents until the discrepancy is physically resolved.

open_questions.question: What keying method and correction history produced the U-M encoding, and which differences from Wright are intentional rather than transcription errors?
next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source package from U-M.
Collate representative XML passages against Wright and Whitelock when lawful full access to the latter is available.
Keep the TEMPHEAD 2016-06-21 date scoped to the documented review.

notes: The preferred title and 1350–1400 composition range describe the Midland redaction, not every Seven Sages version.
The witness's date, production in England, physical extent, and current holding are kept distinct from the work's composition and from Wright's publication place.
Wright's separately issued introduction and notes inform the catalog record but remain editorial apparatus outside the medieval work.
The XML's 3,455-line count and fifteen tale divisions are encoding metrics verified against the immediate edition; the generic printed tale headings are not normalized into manuscript rubrics.
The exact immediate source route before the Midland redactor and the availability of a complete manuscript facsimile remain explicit open questions.
